What's an Algorithm, and Why Should You Know a Little About Them?
This post is a casual chat about what an algorithm is and why you should know a bit about them. It's meant as a beginner-friendly intro, so I won't get into anything too complex.
Before we get into what an algorithm is, we need to talk about two terms:
Why these two? Because algorithms are about providing the best possible solution within a limited amount of time and space — that's why these two come up.
So what's "time"? It's the time it takes to do something. Say writing one post takes you 1 hour — then the "time" here is 1 hour. That's what we mean by time.
"Space" usually refers to memory usage. For example, if an image takes 1MB of memory, the space is 1MB. That's what we mean by space here.
Are algorithms used a lot? Honestly, algorithms are around you all the time. For example, when you're scrolling through a social platform, you've probably seen comments like:

The algorithm brought me here
The algorithm led me to this comment / post
Unless you literally don't use the internet.
So an algorithm is a defined set of instructions or steps a computer can run, which can solve a problem within a finite amount of time and space. That's what we call an algorithm.

If that's too dense, just think of an algorithm as a series of steps that solves a problem. Algorithms aren't limited to computers either — any sequence of steps that solves a problem qualifies as an algorithm.
For a real-life example, take stir-frying water spinach. The steps might be:
Add a reasonable amount of oil
Add a reasonable amount of salt
Wait until the water spinach is cooked
These steps are an algorithm — and this algorithm solves the problem of "stir-fry water spinach".

Of course, this isn't the only algorithm for stir-frying water spinach. You can use other algorithms — shrimp paste water spinach, garlic water spinach, etc. — those are all different algorithms, just with different steps.
Why should you know a little about algorithms?
Now that we have the basic concept, let's talk about why you should know a bit about algorithms.
From here on, all the algorithm discussion is about computers, and I'll mostly use JavaScript as the example language.
We mentioned earlier that an algorithm is a series of steps to achieve a goal. But here we'll layer time and space concepts on top of those goals — meaning, we

Why analyze them? Say you have two algorithms that produce the same result — you'd compare how much time and space each took to get there. That's "time complexity" and "space complexity" in the algorithm world.
Talking algorithm theory in text is a bit abstract, so let's look at some example code instead.

When writing algorithms, we usually analyze which one is the best. How? Using the time complexity and space complexity concepts I mentioned.
Time complexity first: time complexity is how much time the algorithm takes to run. So for the code above, we can use
to measure it. We'll deliberately pass in a big number for
A good algorithm comes down to how much time it takes and how much memory it uses.
But heads up: measuring with a timer isn't actually a good way to analyze algorithm speed. If you call
repeatedly, the time varies each call. Plus, different machines have different specs and performance, so timing isn't a reliable analysis method — it's just a rough reference.

So how do you properly analyze an algorithm's quality? That's where "Big O" comes in. Big O is used to express time and space complexity formally. But getting into Big O here would make this post too complex, so this is really just a casual intro to what algorithms are and why you should know a bit about them.
